destination.name: Ribera del Duero
destination.hero_image_credit: Photo by Matthieu Joannon on Unsplash
destination.hero_image_alt: Ribera del Duero wine country, rolling vineyard plateau under a clear sky
destination.blurb: Spain's premier single-varietal red-wine appellation, where Tinto Fino drives Vega Sicilia Unico and Pingus to global acclaim across a windswept plateau straddling four provinces at 850-900 metres above sea level.
destination.tagline: Tinto Fino on the Castilian plateau, from cooperative roots to cult-wine gravity
destination.overview: DO Ribera del Duero runs 115 kilometres east-west along the Duero river through Burgos, Valladolid, Soria and Segovia provinces, with over 300 wineries producing exclusively from Tinto Fino (the local name for Tempranillo) alongside permitted Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Malbec and Garnacha. The appellation was formally established in 1982, though Vega Sicilia had already been making wine here since 1864. Since Peter Sisseck launched Pingus in 1995 the region has attracted a new generation of growers focused on old-vine Tinto Fino at altitude.

index.title: Ribera del Duero Wine Region Guide | Cork and Curve
index.description: Explore DO Ribera del Duero: Vega Sicilia, Pingus and over 300 bodegas producing old-vine Tinto Fino on the high Castilian plateau of northern Spain.

vineyards.title: Ribera del Duero Vineyards and Bodegas | Cork and Curve
vineyards.description: Top Ribera del Duero bodegas with tasting programmes: Vega Sicilia, Dominio de Pingus, Emilio Moro, Aalto, Pérez Pascuas and Dominio de Atauta.

signature-grapes.title: Ribera del Duero Signature Grapes | Cork and Curve
signature-grapes.description: Ribera del Duero grapes: Tinto Fino dominates, with Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Malbec, Garnacha and the newly permitted Albillo Mayor white.

neighborhoods.title: Ribera del Duero Sub-Zones and Wine Towns | Cork and Curve
neighborhoods.description: Ribera del Duero sub-zones: La Horra and Pedrosa in Burgos, Pesquera and Quintanilla in Valladolid, and the Soria old-vine frontier at Atauta.

tasting-rooms.title: Ribera del Duero Tasting Rooms | Cork and Curve
tasting-rooms.description: Ribera del Duero tasting rooms and cellar visits: Protos at Penafiel, appointment tasting at Vega Sicilia and boutique estate visits across the plateau.

wine-tours.title: Ribera del Duero Wine Tours | Cork and Curve
wine-tours.description: Guided wine tours in Ribera del Duero: Ruta del Vino self-drives, private day tours to Penafiel, La Horra, Pesquera and the Soria old-vine zone.

wine-hotels.title: Ribera del Duero Wine Hotels and Vineyard Stays | Cork and Curve
wine-hotels.description: Ribera del Duero wine hotels and vineyard stays: Arzuaga Navarro vineyard hotel, Hacienda Zorita eco-lodge, Pradorey and Portia estate lodges.

wine-history.title: Ribera del Duero Wine History | Cork and Curve
wine-history.description: Ribera del Duero history: from Vega Sicilia 1864 and Protos 1927 cooperative through the 1982 DO and the cult-wine revolution sparked by Pingus in 1995.

seasonal-wine.title: Ribera del Duero Harvest and Seasonal Wine Guide | Cork and Curve
seasonal-wine.description: Ribera del Duero seasons: late-September harvest on the high plateau, spring bud-break, summer cellar visits and autumn Crianza and Reserva release windows.

wine-festivals.title: Ribera del Duero Wine Festivals | Cork and Curve
wine-festivals.description: Ribera del Duero wine festivals: Aranda de Duero wine fair, Penafiel harvest celebration, regional wine-week events and en-primeur tastings.

faqs.q: What classification does Ribera del Duero hold?
faqs.a: Ribera del Duero is a Denominacion de Origen (DO), Spain's standard quality-wine classification, granted in 1982. It is not a DOCa (Denominacion de Origen Calificada); only Rioja and Priorat hold that higher tier in Spain. All estate wines produced within the boundary and meeting DO regulations are labelled DO Ribera del Duero.

faqs.q: What grape variety dominates Ribera del Duero?
faqs.a: Tinto Fino, the local name for Tempranillo, accounts for over 90 per cent of plantings and is the only variety required in the DO blend. Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Malbec and Garnacha Tinta are permitted as minor blending components up to a maximum percentage set by the regulatory council.

faqs.q: What is the difference between Crianza, Reserva and Gran Reserva in Ribera del Duero?
faqs.a: These are ageing classifications, not vineyard rankings. Ribera del Duero Crianza reds must age at least 24 months with a minimum of 12 in oak. Reserva requires 36 months with at least 12 in oak. Gran Reserva requires 60 months total with at least 24 in oak. Many top estates use these designations; others prefer single-vineyard or brand names.

faqs.q: Where are the best sub-zones for old-vine Tinto Fino?
faqs.a: La Horra and Pedrosa de Duero in Burgos province are considered the heartland for structured old-vine Tinto Fino, home to Pingus, Dominio del Aguila and Hermanos Sastre. The extreme eastern Soria sub-zone around Atauta has the region's oldest ungrafted vines at above 1000 metres, vinified by Dominio de Atauta and a handful of specialists.

faqs.q: Does Ribera del Duero make white wine?
faqs.a: White Ribera del Duero was permitted under the DO regulations from 2019. Albillo Mayor is the primary permitted white variety. A small number of producers such as Aalto, Comenge and Dominio del Aguila now produce white wines under the DO label, though reds remain more than 95 per cent of production.

faqs.q: What is the best way to visit Ribera del Duero from Madrid?
faqs.a: Ribera del Duero is approximately two hours north of Madrid by car via the A-1 motorway to Aranda de Duero, which is the main gateway town. From Aranda, Peñafiel, Pesquera de Duero and the Burgos plateau are all within 30 to 50 minutes. Weekend self-drive itineraries combining two or three estates in a day are the most common format.
